perturbation.original_text: Article 9 – Term and Termination This Agreement enters into force by signing of all Parties and can be terminated by either Party with immediate effect by giving a written notice to the other Party. This Agreement ends automatically upon expiry of the Member’s term of office.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 9 – Term and Termination This Agreement enters into force by signing of all Parties and can be terminated by either Party with immediate effect by giving a written notice to the other Party. This Agreement ends automatically upon expiry of the Member’s term of office, unless the ICC provides written notice of renewal for another term of equal length. The terms of the renewed agreement will be subject to renegotiation.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by stating the agreement ends automatically upon expiry of the member's term of office and then immediately adding an exception for renewal, contradicting the automatic termination clause. This creates uncertainty regarding the actual term of the agreement and under what conditions it terminates. Therefore, the clause on the term and termination is now contradicting itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 9 – Term and Termination This Agreement enters into force by signing of all Parties and can be terminated by either Party with immediate effect by giving a written notice to the other Party. This Agreement ends automatically upon expiry of the Member’s term of office, unless the ICC provides written notice of renewal for another term of equal length. The terms of the renewed agreement will be subject to renegotiation.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Article 9 by adding a clause allowing renewal of the agreement, which directly contradicts the initial statement that the agreement ends automatically upon expiry of the member's term. The contradicted text is the changed text itself, because the change introduces an internal inconsistency within the article regarding the termination of the agreement. The addition of a renewal clause introduces uncertainty about the agreement's termination and directly conflicts with the original statement of automatic termination.

perturbation.original_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Confidential Information or Proprietary Information. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive indefinitely or to the longest extent permitted by the applicable laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Confidential Information or Proprietary Information, except as explicitly permitted by the ICC in writing. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive for a period of five (5) years from the date of termination, notwithstanding any applicable laws.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by first stating the member should stop using information, but adds an exception where ICC may permit them to continue to do so. Furthermore, it modifies the survival of obligations from 'indefinitely' to a fixed period of 'five years', directly contradicting the original indefinite term and creating uncertainty about which term to follow. Thus, creating a contradiction and uncertainty of enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Confidential Information or Proprietary Information, except as explicitly permitted by the ICC in writing. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive for a period of five (5) years from the date of termination, notwithstanding any applicable laws.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a strong in-text contradiction. The original text stated that obligations survive 'indefinitely'. The changed text modifies this to a fixed period of 'five (5) years from the date of termination, notwithstanding any applicable laws'. This creates a direct conflict and inconsistency regarding the duration of the obligations, leading to uncertainty about which term to follow and therefore creates a strong contradiction. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, indicating that the change itself is the source of the contradiction within the document, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Confidential Information or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Confidential Information or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Confidential Information and Proprietary Information with no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information and at least with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Confidential information or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Confidential Information or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Confidential Information or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit, unless required by law or legal process; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Confidential Information and Proprietary Information with no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information and at least with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Confidential information or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by adding 'unless required by law or legal process' to the restriction on using confidential information for the member's benefit. This clause conflicts with the overarching obligation of strict confidentiality detailed in other parts of Article 2, potentially requiring members to disclose information even without ICC Court authorization under legal compulsion. This creates uncertainty because it undermines the confidentiality by introducing legal requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Confidential Information or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Confidential Information or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit, unless required by law or legal process; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Confidential Information and Proprietary Information with no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information and at least with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Confidential information or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds 'unless required by law or legal process' to Article 2(b), which directly contradicts the original intent of strict confidentiality and the obligation to only disclose information with ICC Court authorization, as stated in Article 2(a). The contradicted text is the entire Article 2, particularly 2(a), because the addition creates an exception that undermines the overall confidentiality obligations outlined in the article. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition, as different parts of Article 2 now present conflicting obligations regarding the disclosure of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within thirty (30) days of the request being made.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data, unless otherwise instructed in writing by the ICC Court. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within thirty (30) days of the request being made.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by stating the member should dispose of the information, but adds an exception where ICC may instruct them not to. Disposal is defined as destruction of data, the addition of an exception undermines the obligation to destroy all confidential information by allowing for alternative instructions. This creates uncertainty of whether the disposal needs to be completed or not and also whether it would be an issue.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data, unless otherwise instructed in writing by the ICC Court. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within thirty (30) days of the request being made.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction. The original text states that disposal means destruction of all confidential information. The changed text adds 'unless otherwise instructed in writing by the ICC Court,' which allows for the possibility that the confidential information will not be destroyed, contradicting the initial definition of 'disposal' as destruction. This creates uncertainty and a potential conflict within Article 12 as the Contradicted Text and the Changed Text are in the same location. The Perturbation explanation also aligns with this assessment, and the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction also aligns.

perturbation.original_text: Article 14 – Dispute Resolution This Agreement is governed by, and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of France. Any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved by both Parties or their representatives, if any, through consultation and negotiations at the request of either Party by written notice to the other Party. Failing a resolution within three weeks following the notice mentioned in the above paragraph, the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris shall have exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide any suit, action or proceedings, and to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 14 – Dispute Resolution This Agreement is governed by, and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of France, except for matters concerning intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland. Any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved by both Parties or their representatives, if any, through consultation and negotiations at the request of either Party by written notice to the other Party. Failing a resolution within three weeks following the notice mentioned in the above paragraph, the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris shall have exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide any suit, action or proceedings, and to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, except for intellectual property disputes, which shall be submitted to arbitration in Geneva.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by stating the contract is governed by French law, then creates an exception that matters concerning intellectual property will be governed by Swiss law. Further, it assigns exclusive jurisdiction to the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris, then mandates arbitration in Geneva for intellectual property disputes. This creates uncertainty as to which laws and venue should be used for IP-related disputes, contradicting the original clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 14 – Dispute Resolution This Agreement is governed by, and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of France, except for matters concerning intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland. Any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved by both Parties or their representatives, if any, through consultation and negotiations at the request of either Party by written notice to the other Party. Failing a resolution within three weeks following the notice mentioned in the above paragraph, the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris shall have exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide any suit, action or proceedings, and to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, except for intellectual property disputes, which shall be submitted to arbitration in Geneva.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Article 14 to include an exception for intellectual property disputes, stating they are governed by Swiss law and subject to arbitration in Geneva. This directly contradicts the initial statement that the agreement is governed by French law and that the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris has exclusive jurisdiction for all disputes. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, meaning that within Article 14, there is a direct conflict of law and jurisdiction, creating an in-text contradiction as the same Article states two different set of rules for dispute resolution based on the type of dispute.

perturbation.original_text: Article 7 – No Warranty The ICC Court makes available the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information as is and only warrants that, it has done its best efforts to ensure to the best possible extent that the information disclosed is complete, accurate, free from defects or third party rights, and useful for the purposes of the Member.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 7 – No Warranty The ICC Court makes available the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information as is and only warrants that, it has done its best efforts to ensure to the best possible extent that the information disclosed is complete, accurate, free from defects or third party rights, and useful for the purposes of the Member. However, the ICC Court provides no warranty whatsoever regarding the fitness of the information for any specific purpose or use intended by the Member.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by claiming that the ICC warrants that they have done their best to ensure the information is useful for the purposes of the member, but then immediately asserts that it provides no warranty whatsoever regarding the fitness of the information for any purpose intended by the member. This creates uncertainty as to the extent of the warranty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 7 – No Warranty The ICC Court makes available the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information as is and only warrants that, it has done its best efforts to ensure to the best possible extent that the information disclosed is complete, accurate, free from defects or third party rights, and useful for the purposes of the Member. However, the ICC Court provides no warranty whatsoever regarding the fitness of the information for any specific purpose or use intended by the Member.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a strong in-text contradiction. The original text asserts that the ICC Court 'only warrants that...the information disclosed is...useful for the purposes of the Member.' The changed text adds a clause stating 'However, the ICC Court provides no warranty whatsoever regarding the fitness of the information for any specific purpose or use intended by the Member.' This directly contradicts the original warranty of usefulness, creating uncertainty and a clear conflict within Article 7, where the Changed Text and Contradicted Text are located.
